summary: Italian composer (1567–1643), spanning the Renaissance-to-Baroque transition. Key figure in the development of opera and the madrigal. Composed L'Orfeo (1607), the earliest opera still regularly performed; L'incoronazione di Poppea (1642), his last opera; and the Vespers of 1610. Coined 'prima prattica' and 'seconda prattica' in response to attacks by theorist Giovanni Artusi. Music director of St. Mark's Cathedral in Venice. Eight books of madrigals; developed the stile concitato (agitated style) using pizzicato and tremolo.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Italian composer (1567–1643) working during the Renaissance-Baroque transition. Served as music director of St. Mark's Cathedral in Venice from 1613 until his death. His madrigals were attacked by theorist Giovanni Artusi for their 'licentious' dissonances; Monteverdi and his brother responded by coining the terms 'prima prattica' (first practice) and 'seconda prattica' (second practice). The seconda prattica subordinated counterpoint to textual expression. In 2000, the Monteverdi Choir (founded by Sir John Eliot Gardiner in 1964) embarked on a pilgrimage to dozens of European churches to record Bach's cantatas. At least three Monteverdi libretti were written by Giulio Strozzi, adoptive father of composer Barbara Strozzi. Wikipedia

In his reply to Giovanni Artusi's criticisms, this composer coined the terms prima prattica and seconda prattica to describe how dissonances were prepared.

clues.clue: The seconda prattica (also called stile moderno) emphasized text expression over contrapuntal convention
examples: Claudio Monteverdi coined the name for this style to distinguish his emphasis on the words of pieces, as opposed to the melodic emphasis of earlier composers. It is sometimes also called the stile moderno.
This composer embraced the musical liberty of his output as a facet of what was deemed the 'second practice' by music theorist Giovanni Artusi.

works.name: L'Orfeo
works.description: Opera in five acts, premiered in Mantua in 1607 under the patronage of the Duke of Mantua. The earliest opera still regularly performed. A brass toccata (repeated three times) opens the work before the personification of Music (La Musica) delivers the prologue; that toccata was reused as the 'Deus in adjutorium' opening of the 1610 Vespers. The opera follows Orpheus (Orfeo), who descends to the Underworld to recover Eurydice. In the finale, Apollo descends on a cloud (deus ex machina) to invite Orpheus to heaven — replacing Alessandro Striggio's original ending. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Earliest opera still in regular performance; Orpheus descends to the Underworld for Eurydice; composed 1607 for the Duke of Mantua
examples: name this Italian composer, whose vocal works include eight complete books of madrigals and operas like L'Orfeo
what Baroque composer wrote the earliest opera still regularly performed, L'Orfeo?

works.name: L'incoronazione di Poppea (The Coronation of Poppea)
works.description: Monteverdi's last opera (1642), set in ancient Rome. Deals with Emperor Nero's love for Poppea; Empress Ottavia tries to have Poppea killed. Ends with the love duet 'Pur ti miro, pur ti godo.' First revival in 1651 in Naples. Wikipedia

examples: Monteverdi's last opera is set in this civilization and ends with the duet 'Pur ti miro, pur ti godo.'
Naples was the site of the 1651 first revival of this earlier composer's opera The Coronation of Poppea.

works.name: Vespers of 1610 (Vespro della Beata Vergine)
works.description: Sacred collection published in 1610, dedicated to the Blessed Virgin Mary. Opens with 'Deus in adjutorium' (reusing the toccata from L'Orfeo) followed by a choir with trumpet fanfares. Includes the duet 'Pulchra es' and concludes with a long Magnificat setting. The motet 'Nigra sum' is written for tenor; the two-tenor piece 'Duo Seraphim' adds a third tenor to symbolize the Trinity. Combines old 'first practice' (cantus firmus writing) with the new seconda prattica. Sir John Eliot Gardiner founded the Monteverdi Choir in 1964 specifically to perform it. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with 'Deus in adjutorium' (using the L'Orfeo toccata); includes 'Pulchra es,' 'Duo Seraphim,' and concludes with a Magnificat setting; combines first and second practice
examples: This composition of 1610... includes the duet 'Pulchra es' and concludes with a long setting of the Magnificat.
This composer's 1610 Vespro della Beata Vergine marks the importation of his seconda pratica style into sacred music.

clues.clue: 'Duo Seraphim' — two tenors represent angels, joined by a third tenor to symbolize the Trinity
examples: A piece by this composer contains a movement in which two tenors represent angels and are joined by a third tenor to symbolize the Trinity.

works.name: Madrigals (Eight Books)
works.description: Monteverdi published eight books of madrigals (a ninth was published posthumously). The fifth book (including 'Cruda Amarilli') drew Artusi's attack. The eighth book (1638), subtitled 'Madrigals of War and Love,' includes 'Lamento della ninfa' and 'Il Combattimento di Tancredi e Clorinda,' which pioneered pizzicato and tremolo. Also contained the 'Sestina' inspired by the death of opera singer Caterina Martinelli, opening with 'Burned Remains.' A Sestina in the sixth book includes a nymph's lament with basso ostinato. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Claudio Monteverdi (1567–1643) was an Italian composer who straddled the Renaissance-Baroque divide. He is credited with pioneering the opera and transforming the madrigal. He served as music director of St. Mark's Cathedral in Venice from 1613 until his death, a post previously held by Giovanni Gabrieli.

Monteverdi published eight books of madrigals. His Fifth Book (containing 'Cruda Amarilli' and 'Anima mia, perdona') was attacked by the theorist Giovanni Artusi, who criticized Monteverdi's 'licentious' dissonances. In response, Monteverdi and his brother coined the terms 'prima prattica' (first practice — the old Palestrinian rules) and 'seconda prattica' (second practice — dissonance subordinated to text expression, also called stile moderno). Artusi's anonymous attacks called Monteverdi's unpublished madrigals 'castles in the air, chimeras founded upon sand.'

Monteverdi's Eighth Book of Madrigals (1638), subtitled 'of War and Love,' is notable for two landmark works. 'Il Combattimento di Tancredi e Clorinda' depicts a battle between a Christian knight and a Saracen warrior woman, pioneering the stile concitato (agitated style) through novel uses of pizzicato and tremolo — among the earliest in orchestral music. 'Lamento della ninfa' features a solo soprano who sings 'at the tempo of her emotion' over a basso ostinato ground in A minor (descending fourth), while three men sing in strict time.

L'Orfeo (1607), composed for the Duke of Mantua, is the earliest opera still regularly performed. It opens with a brass toccata (a trumpet fanfare repeated three times), which Monteverdi reused as the 'Deus in adjutorium meum intende' opening of his 1610 Vespers. The personification of Music (La Musica) delivers the prologue; her ritornello is an early operatic leitmotif. The opera's vocal highlight is 'Possente spirto,' Orpheus's aria as he attempts to persuade Charon to ferry him across the Styx — Monteverdi wrote two alternative vocal lines for the soloist and included prominent call-and-response with a trumpet. The original ending (by librettist Alessandro Striggio the Younger, in which the Bacchae murder Orpheus) was replaced by a deus ex machina finale in which Apollo descends on a cloud to take Orpheus to heaven.

The Vespers of 1610 (Vespro della Beata Vergine) opens with 'Deus in adjutorium' (the repurposed L'Orfeo toccata), includes the tenor-voice motet 'Nigra sum,' the two-tenor 'Duo Seraphim' (a third tenor added to symbolize the Trinity), the duet 'Pulchra es,' and concludes with a large Magnificat setting. It combines cantus firmus writing (the old first practice) with the new seconda prattica. Sir John Eliot Gardiner founded the Monteverdi Choir in 1964 specifically to perform this work.

Monteverdi's final opera, L'incoronazione di Poppea (1642), set in ancient Rome, ends with the duet 'Pur ti miro, pur ti godo' between Nero and Poppea. The first revival was in Naples in 1651. At least three of Monteverdi's libretti were written by Giulio Strozzi, the adoptive father of composer Barbara Strozzi, who carried forward the seconda prattica style. Gian Francesco Malipiero created the first complete edition of Monteverdi's works between 1926 and 1942.

cards.front: Composer: Italian; composed L'Orfeo (the earliest opera still in regular performance), The Coronation of Poppea, and the 1610 Vespers
Composer: Giovanni Artusi attacked his madrigals for 'licentious' dissonance; he responded by coining 'prima prattica' and 'seconda prattica'
Composer: music director of St. Mark's Cathedral in Venice from 1613; Sir John Eliot Gardiner founded the Monteverdi Choir in 1964 to perform his 1610 Vespers
Opera: opens with a brass toccata (trumpet fanfare repeated three times) before the personification of Music delivers a prologue; Orpheus descends to retrieve Eurydice
Opera: its opening toccata was reused as the 'Deus in adjutorium' opening of the 1610 Vespers
Opera: 'Possente spirto' — Orpheus's aria to persuade Charon; includes trumpet call-and-response; two alternative vocal lines were written for the soloist
Opera: the original librettist Alessandro Striggio's ending (the Bacchae murder Orpheus) was replaced by an Apollo deus ex machina finale; a 2019 Budapest production restored Striggio's ending
Opera: Monteverdi's last opera; set in ancient Rome; Empress Ottavia tries to have Poppea killed; ends with the duet 'Pur ti miro, pur ti godo'; first revived in Naples in 1651
cards.work: L'incoronazione di Poppea (The Coronation of Poppea)

cards.front: Sacred work: opens with 'Deus in adjutorium' (reusing the L'Orfeo toccata); includes 'Pulchra es,' 'Duo Seraphim' (two tenors joined by a third for the Trinity), and a large Magnificat setting
Madrigal: 'Il Combattimento di Tancredi e Clorinda' (from the 8th book) — depicts battle between Christian knight and Saracen woman; pioneered pizzicato and tremolo as the stile concitato
Madrigal: 'Lamento della ninfa' (1638, 8th book) — soprano sings 'at the tempo of her emotion' over a descending-fourth ground bass in A minor; three men sing in strict time

recursive_suggestions: L'Orfeo (Monteverdi) — the toccata, 'Possente spirto,' and the plot deserve deeper individual clue analysis
Barbara Strozzi — appears as a contextual mention multiple times in Monteverdi questions; her father Giulio wrote Monteverdi libretti
Giovanni Artusi — the theorist who attacked Monteverdi's madrigals is a recurrent character in AFA questions about the seconda prattica
